title: The man who raises the bridge at night
summary: A night-shift bridge keeper on a small canal in Hamburg describes the single barge he raises the bridge for each night, and why a job that machines could do is still done by a person.
body:
My shift starts at ten in the evening and ends at six, and for most of those eight hours nothing happens. I sit in a small glass cabin above a canal in eastern Hamburg, with a kettle, a radio, and a book I have been reading for three weeks because I keep falling asleep over it. The bridge below me is old, made of green iron, and it lifts in two halves like a drawbridge. In a whole night I lift it once.

The barge is called the Margarethe, and she passes at about half past two, carrying gravel up to the harbour. Her captain, Olsen, has been making the same run for nineteen years. I have never met him properly; we have a system instead. When his light appears around the bend, I switch my cabin lamp off and on twice. He answers with one long blast. I raise the bridge, he passes underneath, he raises a hand I can only just see, and I lower the bridge. It takes four minutes.

People ask me why this is a job. The bridge could lift itself. A sensor could see the barge, a motor could raise the iron, and a camera could check that no late cyclist is crossing. It would be cheaper, and it would never fall asleep over a book. The machine would do nine nights out of ten perfectly well.

It is the tenth night that I am paid for. Last winter the canal froze at the hinges and the bridge would not lift; I went down with hot water and a bar to work it free. Once a drunk man sat down on the rising bridge for a joke, and I stopped it with my hand on the lever a second before it would have tipped him into the canal. A sensor does not know that the shape on the bridge is a man and not a shadow. I do.

I have come to think of the Margarethe as the only fixed point in my night. If she is late, I worry; if she is early, I know something has changed upriver. Olsen and I have never had a conversation, and yet I know him: I know he slows down in fog, that he is faster in summer, that the night his blast came twice instead of once was the night, I learned later, that his wife had died. I raised the bridge that night the way you would hold a door for someone carrying something heavy.

When I retire, they will almost certainly automate the bridge, and they will be right to. I will not argue. But I hope whoever signs that order understands what they are removing. It is not a man who lifts a bridge. It is a man who has watched one stretch of water so long that he can read it, and who is there, on the tenth night, when the water does something the machine was never told to expect.

annotations.meaningVi: Ca của tôi bắt đầu lúc mười giờ
cạy / làm cho nó rời ra (không kẹt nữa)

annotations.phrase: a man and not a shadow
annotations.meaningVi: một con người chứ không phải một cái bóng
đang bê thứ gì đó nặng

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for duration + second/third conditionals
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng hiện tại hoàn thành chỉ khoảng thời gian kéo dài tới hiện tại: 'a book I have been reading for three weeks', 'who has been making the same run for nineteen years', 'I have come to think of the Margarethe as the only fixed point'. Bài cũng dùng câu điều kiện loại 3 cho quá khứ giả định: 'a second before it would have tipped him into the canal' (would have + V3 — điều đã suýt xảy ra nhưng không xảy ra), và điều kiện loại 2 giả định hiện tại: 'It would be cheaper, and it would never fall asleep'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The text uses the present perfect for duration up to now: 'a book I have been reading for three weeks', 'who has been making the same run for nineteen years', 'I have come to think of the Margarethe as the only fixed point'. It also uses the 3rd conditional for the hypothetical past: 'a second before it would have tipped him into the canal' (would have + V3 — what almost happened but did not), and the 2nd conditional for the hypothetical present: 'It would be cheaper, and it would never fall asleep'.

translationVi:
Ca của tôi bắt đầu lúc mười giờ tối và kết thúc lúc sáu giờ sáng. Phần lớn tám tiếng đó, chẳng có gì xảy ra. Tôi ngồi trong một ca-bin kính nhỏ phía trên một con kênh ở phía đông Hamburg, với một ấm nước, một cái radio, và một cuốn sách tôi đọc đã ba tuần vì cứ ngủ gật trên nó. Cây cầu phía dưới đã cũ, làm bằng sắt sơn xanh, và nó nâng lên thành hai nửa như một cây cầu rút. Cả một đêm tôi chỉ nâng nó một lần.

Chiếc sà lan tên là Margarethe, và nó đi qua vào khoảng hai giờ rưỡi, chở sỏi lên cảng. Thuyền trưởng, Olsen, đã chạy cùng một chuyến mười chín năm. Tôi chưa bao giờ gặp ông ấy đàng hoàng; thay vào đó chúng tôi có một quy ước. Khi đèn của ông hiện ra ở khúc cua, tôi tắt rồi bật đèn ca-bin hai lần. Ông đáp lại bằng một hồi còi dài. Tôi nâng cầu, ông đi qua bên dưới, ông giơ một bàn tay mà tôi chỉ vừa kịp thấy, và tôi hạ cầu xuống lại. Việc đó mất bốn phút.

Người ta hỏi tôi tại sao đây lại là một công việc. Cây cầu có thể tự nâng. Một cảm biến có thể thấy sà lan; một động cơ có thể nâng phần sắt; và một camera có thể kiểm tra không có người đi xe đạp muộn nào đang băng qua. Sẽ rẻ hơn, và nó sẽ không bao giờ ngủ gật trên một cuốn sách. Máy sẽ làm tốt chín đêm trên mười.

Chính đêm thứ mười là cái mà tôi được trả lương. Mùa đông năm ngoái con kênh đóng băng ở bản lề và cầu không nâng được; tôi xuống với nước nóng và một thanh sắt để cạy cho nó rời ra. Có lần một người say ngồi xuống cây cầu đang nâng để đùa, và tôi đã dừng nó lại bằng tay đặt trên cần gạt một giây trước khi nó hất anh ta xuống kênh. Một cảm biến không biết rằng hình thù trên cầu là một con người chứ không phải một cái bóng. Tôi thì biết.

Tôi đã đi đến chỗ coi chiếc Margarethe là điểm cố định duy nhất trong đêm của mình. Nếu nó muộn, tôi lo; nếu nó sớm, tôi biết có gì đó đã thay đổi ở thượng nguồn. Olsen và tôi chưa từng trò chuyện, vậy mà tôi biết ông: tôi biết ông đi chậm lại trong sương mù, biết ông nhanh hơn vào mùa hè, biết cái đêm còi của ông vang hai lần thay vì một lần là đêm mà, sau này tôi mới hay, vợ ông qua đời. Đêm đó tôi nâng cầu theo cái cách bạn giữ cửa cho một người đang bê thứ gì đó nặng.

Khi tôi về hưu, gần như chắc chắn họ sẽ tự động hóa cây cầu, và họ làm vậy là đúng. Tôi sẽ không tranh cãi. Nhưng tôi mong người ký lệnh đó hiểu họ đang bỏ đi cái gì. Đó không phải một người nâng một cây cầu. Đó là một người đã canh một khúc nước lâu đến mức có thể đọc được nó, và là người có mặt, vào đêm thứ mười, khi mặt nước làm điều mà máy chưa từng được bảo là phải lường trước.

questions.prompt: When does the keeper's shift run, and how often does he lift the bridge?
options: Ten at night to six in the morning; he lifts the bridge once in a whole night
All day; many times an hour
questions.explanation: 'My shift starts at ten in the evening and ends at six... In a whole night I lift it once.'

questions.prompt: What does the barge Margarethe carry, and how long has Olsen run the route?
options: Gravel to a building site near the harbour; nineteen years
questions.explanation: 'carrying gravel up to a building site near the harbour. Her captain is a man named Olsen who has been making the same run for nineteen years.'

questions.prompt: What is the signal system between the keeper and Olsen?
options: The keeper flicks his lamp off and on twice; Olsen answers with one long blast
questions.explanation: 'I switch my cabin lamp off and on twice. He answers with one long blast.'

questions.prompt: Why does the keeper admit a machine could do most of the work?
options: A sensor could see the barge and a motor lift the iron; it would be cheaper and never fall asleep — fine on nine nights out of ten
questions.explanation: 'It would be cheaper, and it would never fall asleep... the machine would do nine nights out of ten perfectly well.'

questions.prompt: What does the keeper mean by 'the tenth night'?
options: The unusual night when something happens a machine was not told to expect — the night he is really paid for
Every tenth day of the month
His tenth year on the job
questions.explanation: 'It is the tenth night that I am paid for.'

questions.prompt: Which of these actually happened on the bridge?
options: The canal froze at the hinges, and a drunk man sat down on the rising bridge for a joke
questions.explanation: The fourth paragraph describes the frozen hinges and the drunk man on the rising bridge.

questions.prompt: What did the keeper do when the drunk man sat on the bridge?
options: He stopped it with his hand on the lever a second before it would have tipped the man into the canal
He woke the man with the horn
questions.explanation: 'I stopped it with my hand on the lever a second before it would have tipped him into the canal.'

questions.prompt: How does the keeper know Olsen without ever talking to him?
options: By reading his habits — he slows in fog, is faster in summer, and his blast came twice the night his wife died
questions.explanation: 'I know that he slows down in fog out of caution, that he is faster in summer, that the night his blast came twice... was the night... his wife had died.'

questions.prompt: How did the keeper raise the bridge the night Olsen's blast came twice?
options: The way you hold a door for someone carrying something heavy
Faster than usual to save time
Without looking up from his book
questions.explanation: 'I raised the bridge that night the way you would hold a door for someone carrying something heavy.'

questions.prompt: What is the keeper's attitude to the bridge being automated after he retires?
options: He accepts it and will not argue, but hopes whoever orders it understands what is being removed
He is furious and will fight it
He does not care at all
questions.explanation: 'they will almost certainly automate the bridge, and they will be right to. I will not argue. But I hope that whoever signs that order understands what they are removing.'

questions.prompt: In context, 'work it free' (fourth paragraph) means he did what to the frozen bridge?
options: Loosened it so it would move again
questions.explanation: 'I had to go down with hot water and a bar and work it free' — i.e. free the frozen mechanism so it could lift.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: A task machines could mostly do is kept human because a person who has watched one place for years can read it and act in the rare moment no machine was prepared for.
Night work is lonely and pointless.
Old workers always resist new technology.
questions.explanation: The whole piece, especially 'It is not a man who lifts a bridge. It is a man who has watched one stretch of water for so long that he can read it.'

I sit in a small glass {{b1}} above a canal in eastern Hamburg, with a kettle, a radio, and a book I have been reading for three weeks because I keep falling asleep over it. A sensor does not know that the shape on the bridge is a man and not a shadow. I do.

I have come to think of the Margarethe as the only fixed {{b8}} in my night. If she is late, I worry; if she is early, I know something has changed {{b9}}. Olsen and I have never had a conversation, and yet I know him: I know he slows down in fog, that he is faster in summer, that the night his blast came twice instead of once was the night, I learned later, that his wife had died. It is not a man who lifts a bridge. It is a man who has watched one stretch of water so long that he can read it, and who is there, on the tenth night, when the water does something the machine was never told to expect.

blanks.explanation: A glass cabin above the canal.
It lifts in two halves like a drawbridge.
Carrying gravel up to the harbour.
He answers with one long blast.
A sensor could see the barge.
Work it free — loosen the frozen mechanism.
My hand on the lever stopped the bridge.
The only fixed point in my night.
The way you would hold a door for someone.
They will almost certainly automate the bridge.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The keeper is paid for the rare 'tenth night', not the nine ordinary ones. Think of a role in your own life — a parent, a teacher, a friend — whose real value shows up only in the rare moment things go wrong. How would you measure that value if you only counted the ordinary days?
